questions.statement: <p dir="ltr" style="line-height:1.38;margin-top:0pt;margin-bottom:0pt;"><span id="docs-internal-guid-89b228d4-7fff-d431-6f64-b41b45e60326"><span style="  background-color: transparent; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variant-emoji: normal; vertical-align: baseline; white-space-collapse: preserve;">If <span class="math-tex">\(U\)</span> and <span class="math-tex">\(W\)</span> are distinct 4 - dimensional subspaces of a vector space <span class="math-tex">\(V\)</span> of dimension 6, then the possible dimensions of </span></span><span class="math-tex">\(U \cap W\)</span><span><span style="  background-color: transparent; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variant-emoji: normal; vertical-align: baseline; white-space-collapse: preserve;"> is:</span></span></p>

questions.statement: For n &ne; m let T<sub>1</sub>&nbsp;:&nbsp;ℝ<sup>n</sup>&nbsp;&rarr;&nbsp;ℝ<sup>m</sup> and T<sub>2</sub>&nbsp;:&nbsp;ℝ<sup>m</sup>&nbsp;&rarr;&nbsp;ℝ<sup>n</sup>&nbsp;be two linear transformations such that T<sub>1</sub>T<sub>2</sub> is bejective. Then
Let S = {T : ℝ<sup>3&nbsp;</sup>&rarr; ℝ<sup>3</sup>; T is a linear transformation with T(1, 0, 1) = (1, 2, 3) and T(1 ,2, 3) = (1, 0, 1)}. Then S is
options: a finite set containing more than one element /
